entry.title: Re: DRC for France einvoicing B2G and B2B
entry.content:
A note was created after this post: 3740186 - Electronic Invoicing for France via Approved Platform: Corrections (This note contains a report that will create missing entries : NOTE_3740186_FR_EXT_DATA)

FORM update_01. " TABU EDOEXTDOCTYPE ( EN , source: ERY )
  TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_edoextdoctype,
          land(2),
          ext_doc_type(30),
          ext_doc_sub_type(30),
          ext_doc_type_code(4),
          process(10),
          edoc_type(10),
        END OF ty_edoextdoctype.
  DATA: lt_edoextdoctype TYPE TABLE OF ty_edoextdoctype.
  DATA: lt_edoextdoctype_h LIKE lt_edoextdoctype.
  lt_edoextdoctype_h = value #(    "#EC NOTEXT
  ( land = `FR` ext_doc_type = `CUSTOMER_CREDIT_MEMO` ext_doc_sub_type = `B2B` ext_doc_type_code = `381` process = `FRCINV` edoc_typ
e = `FR_B2BCRE` )
  ( land = `FR` ext_doc_type = `CUSTOMER_INVOICE` ext_doc_sub_type = `B2B` ext_doc_type_code = `380` process = `FRCINV` edoc_type =
`FR_B2BINV` )
  ). APPEND LINES OF lt_edoextdoctype_h TO lt_edoextdoctype.

  CALL METHOD lcl_wb=>create_table_entries "#EC *
    EXPORTING
      i_tabname        = 'EDOEXTDOCTYPE'
      it_entries       = lt_edoextdoctype.

ENDFORM.

entry.title: SAP Public Cloud Label printing with templates stored on printer
entry.content:
Hey dear SAP-Community,

as a task I got to lookup how Label printing works in the cloud.
The customer wants to use a printer where label templates are stored directly on the printer.

I tried it with Integration Suite but there the problem is the printer being behind a firewall.
There I wanted to map/transform the received print data to ZPL format and then send it to the printer.

For me the only working solution I found regarding Public Cloud is creating an Adobe Forms Template and using Print Service + Print Manager to send file to printer.
The problem here is the creation of Adobe Forms Template as they don't want to do that.

Is there any way to use templates stored on printer in Public Cloud 2602 without creating Adobe Forms? - I mostly get to see "older" On-Prem posts.

Thanks and best regards
Luca

entry.title: Is there a way to control TECO using the Authorization Key along with the Phase Control BAdI?
entry.content:
We have configured an Authorization Key (ZBLOCK) assigned to Maintenance Planners and Maintenance Managers to allow TECO execution on maintenance orders, with the intention of restricting Supervisors.

While the Authorization Key supports manual phase control activation/deactivation, it does not allow mass updates via the Find Maintenance Order app, resulting in manual effort.

To address this, we implemented a Phase Control BAdI that automatically deactivates phase control after time confirmation. However, this approach unintentionally allows Supervisors to perform TECO, which we want to restrict.

A key limitation is that the Authorization Key is not available within the PCC BAdI, preventing role-based control in the enhancement logic. Additionally, no restriction field for order status (TECO) is available in Business Roles to control this at role level.

Requirement

  • Allow TECO execution only for Maintenance Planners and Managers

  • Restrict TECO for Supervisors, including in mass processing scenarios (e.g., Find Maintenance Order app)

  • Achieve this through either:

    • Mass deactivation of Phase Control for multiple maintenance orders in one step, or
    • Automatic Phase Control deactivation via BAdI, while still ensuring TECO is restricted at role or app level to only authorized users (Supervisors)

Could anyone advise, how to achieve this? We are using S4 Hana Public Cloud with 2 System Landscape.

entry.title: Re: Output Parameters for Handling Unit
entry.content:
Hello

After opening an SAP incident, I received the following feedback:

On one hand, SAP confirmed that there is currently no application, trace, or log available to analyze the message determination process for HU label printing. For this reason, I have created an Influence Request to propose this enhancement. I am sharing the link below in case you would like to review it and consider voting for it. Improvement Request Details - Customer Influence

On the other hand, SAP suggested opening a new incident under component SCM-EWM-PRN-HU-2CL, describing the specific business requirement in detail. Their recommendation is that the support team for this component may be able to provide guidance on the appropriate configuration or suggest an alternative solution to achieve the desired behavior.

Regards

entry.title: nationalId from SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central to Microsoft Entra ID
entry.content:
Hi experts

We try to get nationalId synced to Microsoft Entra ID through the SuccessFactors, but are having a hard time getting the JSON path API Expression correct. 
We have extracted the value through Postman, using this query:
api55.sapsf.eu/odata/v2/EmpEmployment?$format=JSON&$expand=personNav,personNav/nationalIdNav&$filter=personIdExternal eq 'XXXXXXXX'

$.personNav.nationalIdNav.results[?(@.cardType == nationalId)].nationalId
$.personNav.nationalIdNav..results[?(@.emplStatusNav.externalCode == 'A' || @.emplStatusNav.externalCode == 'U' || @.emplStatusNav.externalCode == 'P')])].nationalId
$.personNav.nationalIdNav.results[0].nationalId
$.nationalIdNav.results[?(@.cardType == 'BN')].nationalId

none of the return any value. I reckon permissions should be okay as we are able to retrieve in OData API with Postman. 

Have anybody been able to retrieve this value?

entry.title: Why Hypercare Success Should Be Measured Before Go-Live, Not After
entry.content:
Why Hypercare Success Should Be Measured Before Go-Live, Not After

In many SAP S/4HANA transformation programs, hypercare is often viewed as a post-go-live activity. Teams complete testing, execute cutover, move into production, and then rely on hypercare to stabilize operations. While this approach is common, I believe it overlooks a critical reality: successful hypercare begins long before go-live.

Organizations frequently judge hypercare based on how quickly issues are resolved after production deployment. However, the true measure of hypercare success is how well the organization prepares for it before the first transaction is processed in the new system.

Over the years, I have seen SAP transformation programs invest significant effort in technical deployment activities while spending relatively little time preparing support teams, business users, escalation processes, and operational readiness models. When that happens, hypercare becomes reactive rather than proactive.

Understanding the Purpose of Hypercare

Hypercare exists to provide enhanced support immediately after go-live. During this period, project teams, business stakeholders, support organizations, and technical experts work closely together to stabilize operations and address issues quickly.

The goal is not simply to resolve incidents. The goal is to maintain business continuity while users transition to new processes, systems, and ways of working.

When hypercare is planned properly, users gain confidence in the new environment. When it is poorly planned, small issues can quickly become operational disruptions.

Why Measuring Hypercare Only After Go-Live Is Too Late

Many organizations track metrics such as:

  • Number of incidents

  • Average resolution time

  • Severity of defects

  • User support requests

  • System performance issues

These metrics are valuable, but they are lagging indicators. They only become visible after go-live has already occurred.

A more effective approach is to measure readiness indicators before production deployment. By doing so, organizations can identify weaknesses early and reduce the likelihood of major disruptions during hypercare.

Illustrative example of a hypercare readiness dashboard showing readiness indicators across people, process, technical, and business dimensions before go-live. Sample metrics are provided for demonstration purposes only.

The Four Dimensions of Hypercare Readiness

In my experience, successful hypercare depends on four key readiness dimensions.

Illustrative framework showing the four critical dimensions of hypercare readiness: People, Process, Technical, and Business Readiness. All four dimensions should be evaluated before go-live to support a stable and successful hypercare period.

1. People Readiness

Support teams should understand business processes, escalation paths, and expected support responsibilities.

Key questions include:

  • Are support teams trained?

  • Are business super users identified?

  • Are escalation contacts documented?

  • Are support schedules established?

Without these foundations, issue resolution often becomes slow and inconsistent.

2. Process Readiness

Organizations should establish clear procedures for incident management, prioritization, communication, and decision-making.

Questions to consider:

  • How will incidents be classified?

  • Who approves emergency fixes?

  • How will business updates be communicated?

  • What governance structure exists during hypercare?

Clear processes reduce confusion when issues arise.

3. Technical Readiness

Technical teams should validate monitoring tools, system alerts, integration tracking, and performance monitoring before go-live.

Examples include:

  • Interface monitoring

  • Batch job monitoring

  • Data reconciliation checks

  • Performance dashboards

  • Security monitoring

Strong technical visibility allows teams to identify issues before users report them.

4. Business Readiness

Business readiness is often the most overlooked area.

Even when the technology works correctly, users may struggle with new processes, approvals, or reporting methods.

Organizations should evaluate:

  • User confidence levels

  • Training completion rates

  • Business process understanding

  • Availability of business support resources

Successful adoption often depends as much on people as technology.

Building a Hypercare Readiness Score

One practical approach is to create a readiness score before go-live.

Each readiness dimension can be evaluated using objective criteria and weighted according to business priorities.

For example:

Readiness Area Example Evaluation

PeopleTraining completion and support coverage
ProcessEscalation workflows and governance
TechnicalMonitoring and system validation
BusinessUser adoption and operational preparedness

The resulting score provides leadership with a fact-based view of hypercare readiness rather than relying on assumptions.

How AI Can Improve Hypercare Planning

AI is increasingly helping organizations move from reactive support models to predictive support models.

Illustrative example showing how AI can analyze testing defects, migration errors, user adoption trends, and operational data to predict post-go-live risks and improve hypercare readiness.

By analyzing:

  • Testing defects

  • Migration errors

  • User training performance

  • Historical incident patterns

  • Process bottlenecks

AI can help identify areas likely to generate support issues after go-live.

This allows teams to focus resources where they are needed most before production deployment occurs.

Rather than waiting for incidents to happen, organizations can proactively reduce risk.

Governance Matters

Hypercare should not operate independently from program governance.

Illustrative governance framework showing how readiness reviews, incident management, executive oversight, and operational transition activities work together to support a stable and successful hypercare period.

Executive stakeholders should have visibility into:

  • Readiness scores

  • Open risks

  • Critical dependencies

  • Escalation trends

  • Business adoption indicators

Regular readiness reviews before go-live help leadership make informed decisions regarding deployment timing and support planning.

The goal is not simply to launch on schedule. The goal is to launch successfully.

A Practical Takeaway

In many SAP transformation programs, hypercare is treated as the safety net that catches issues after go-live. I believe the most successful programs view hypercare differently.

They measure readiness before deployment, establish strong support models, validate operational processes, and ensure that business teams are prepared for change.

When organizations focus on hypercare readiness before go-live, the post-go-live period becomes more predictable, less disruptive, and significantly more successful.

Ultimately, hypercare success is not determined by how many incidents are resolved after go-live. It is determined by how effectively the organization prepares before go-live ever begins.

entry.title: 2026年第一季度产品亮点—SAP 集成套件
entry.content:
本文为译文版本。请在此查看英文原文。

请注意：SAP Cloud ALM 和 Advanced Event Mesh 目前在中国暂不可用。

欢迎阅读 SAP Integration Suite 最新发布功能的又一季度回顾！2026 年第一季度的新能力聚焦于提升运营效率和改进事件驱动机制，旨在帮助每天构建、运行和故障排除集成的从业者更加顺畅地开展工作。欢迎浏览本次亮点，并查看相关文档和演示以获取操作细节，同时参考路线图了解完整功能列表。

增强版现已发布
SAP Integration Suite 增强版通过提供受管控的实时连接、低代码开发工具以及 AI 驱动的运营能力（如 Text-to-Flow、脚本优化、API 异常检测、API 流量预测，以及连接器、事件网格、Document AI、告警和传输管理），帮助将 AI 和智能代理 AI 从试点阶段推进到生产环境。它能够减少集成技术债务，在云和本地系统之间实现安全的智能代理操作，并创建可复用的构建模块，从而更快、更可重复地产生 AI 价值。

加速您的 AI 计划并使其具备企业级能力，详情请参阅 SAP Discovery Center。

事件驱动集成

在 Developer Hub 中发现并发布高级事件网格事件
内容管理员可以直接在 Developer Hub 中发现并发布 Advanced Event Mesh 事件，从而将事件以事件产品的形式对外提供，并帮助应用开发人员更轻松地搜索和找到相关事件。要使用该功能，需要创建一个目标（destination），将 SAP Integration Suite 的 Advanced Event Mesh 连接到 Developer Hub；连接完成后，相关业务系统将显示在 Developer Hub 中以供发现和发布。

更多详细说明，请参阅文档：在 Developer Hub 中发现并发布 Advanced Event Mesh 事件。

 

从 SAP Event Mesh 迁移到 SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh 的支持
此次更新提供了技术和流程方面的支持，帮助客户从 SAP Event Mesh（默认方案）迁移到 SAP Integration Suite 的 Advanced Event Mesh，使企业能够将其事件驱动架构迁移到更强大、功能更丰富的平台。该指南面向已在使用 SAP Event Mesh 默认方案的客户，概述了扩展其基于事件架构的步骤。阅读指南：从 SAP Event Mesh 迁移到 SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh。

流程集成

Edge Integration Cell 对 Valkey 的支持
SAP Integration Suite 中的 Edge Integration Cell 功能现在支持将 Valkey 作为 Redis 和 HANA 数据库之外的另一种数据存储选项。该选项可在安装时选择，支持 8.0–8.2 版本，使管理员可以在部署中选择 Valkey，从而帮助降低总体拥有成本。

有关步骤和前提条件，请参阅：部署 Edge Integration Cell 解决方案。

在 SAP Focused Run 和 SAP Cloud ALM 上监控消息处理
用户可以通过基于推送的方式，将日志发送至 SAP Focused Run（FRUN）和 SAP Cloud ALM（CALM），以监控来自 Edge Integration Cell 的消息处理。这使得在单一应用中集中监控多个 Edge Integration Cell 运行时成为可能，可以基于消息处理日志状态配置告警，并支持在离线场景下仍通过 SAP Focused Run 监控相关日志。

更多信息请参阅：在 SAP Focused Run 和 SAP Cloud ALM 上监控消息处理。

消息队列管理：在消息队列监控器中扩展和筛选场景详情
消息队列监控器现在显示特定于场景的属性，例如发送方（Sender）、接收方（Receiver）、应用消息类型（Application Message Type）、应用消息 ID（Application Message ID）和关联 ID（Correlation ID）。这些属性可以在消息表设置中启用，并用作筛选条件。

该功能允许您更高效地缩小消息范围，并快速识别问题条目（带有即时上下文），通过直接筛选相关消息缩短问题解决时间；支持更精确的故障排查和根因分析；通过仅显示关键消息来减少运营噪音；通过可见且可搜索的元数据提高审计与合规性；并简化运维、支持和开发团队之间的协作与交接。开始使用：管理存储在队列中的消息。

支持适配器类型的参数化映射
SAP Process Orchestration 导入到 SAP Integration Suite - Cloud Integration 的导入选项已扩展，支持在消息映射中为适配器类别定义参数，使您能够导入包含适配器类型参数的 PI/PO 消息映射；迁移工具和独立的消息映射导入现在可接受含有“Import”类型适配器参数类别的映射对象，从而保留适配器特定参数配置，减少迁移过程中的手动调整和返工，提高迁移映射的准确性，并通过保持与适配器参数相关的运行时行为来加速上手和故障排查。参见文档：从企业服务资源库（ESR）导入消息映射。

连接性更新

客户自管云连接器
Edge Integration Cell 运行时现在支持使用由客户自行管理的外部 SAP Cloud Connector，而非内置的 SAP 管理连接器，使组织可以将连接器部署在最符合其基础设施和安全需求的网络区域。这一功能增加了部署灵活性，提高了安全控制能力，支持额外的流量检查功能，并有助于满足基础设施与合规要求。参见配置步骤：为 Edge Integration Cell 配置外部 Cloud Connector。

此外，用户现在还可以通过 Edge 生命周期管理添加并初始化带有外部 Cloud Connector 的 Edge Node。参见：使用外部 Cloud Connector 初始化 Edge Node。

最新适配器
2026 年第一季度发布的适配器包括：

  • BambooHR 适配器：连接到 BambooHR，以访问员工、组织、休假等相关 API。
  • Zendesk 适配器：为工单、附件、请求、标签等多种实体提供完整的 CRUD 操作。该适配器用于接收和处理 SAP Integration Suite 中集成流程的数据，并通过基于 REST 的 API 与 Zendesk（外部）应用通信。
  • Microsoft 365 Outlook 适配器：通过改善通信并在两个平台之间自动化工作流，实现 Microsoft Outlook 与 SAP Integration Suite 的无缝集成。支持作为发送方和接收方适配器。🎥 观看演示。
  • OFTP2 适配器：使用 Odette 文件传输协议（OFTP）适配器，在 Edge Integration Cell 上与合作伙伴交换 EDI 和业务文件。
  • NFS 文件适配器：支持连接到 NFS 服务器，实现对 Edge Integration Cell 上文件的读写操作。🎥 观看演示。

数据中心可用性更新
SAP Integration Suite 新增的数据中心可用性选项包括：

  • 澳大利亚（悉尼）—SAP 基础设施
  • 日本（东京）和美国—SAP 托管基础设施
  • 英国—通过 Microsoft Azure 提供

进一步了解
想提前了解 SAP Integration Suite 在 2026 年的更多规划？观看该演示：

你最期待在2026年看到哪些关键创新？欢迎在评论区告诉我！

entry.title: Re: How to configure business goals that can change in time
entry.content: In our case, it is understood that one employee has a Goal (e.g., A_Goal*), which has different weights based on various sections. Together, these sections must total 100%. It cannot happen that an employee has, for example, A_Goal1 and A_Goal2 and then B_Goal1 and B_Goal2, etc. It should be understood that the labels A_Goal 1 - 8 are actually one Goal, but with 8 sections and a combined weight of 100%, which is why it is not clear to me how to set it up.

entry.title: Re: Deletion of Obsolete Tables
entry.content:
SODH : 

With SAP S/4HANA release 2023 the new class-based obsolete data handling tool is shipped and replaces the old tool described in SAP Note 2661837

but looks like you are listing technical tables  : 2388483 - How-To: Data Management for Technical Tables

FAGLFLEXT_BCK  is handled by FINS_MIG_OBSOLETE_DB_DEL

HOST_SQL_PLAN_CACHE_BASE  is a HANA internal table

entry.title: Re: when is my alm expiring?
entry.content:
The SAP Cloud ALM entitlement will only expire if ALL Cloud subscriptions expire for the Customer. If even a single Cloud subscription is still valid, it maintains the entitlement to SAP Cloud ALM. 

For more details, please refer to SAP KBA: 

3164352 - How is an SAP Cloud ALM entitlement effected by expiring Cloud subscriptions or expiring Rise with SAP contract?

entry.title: Re: How to Display Position Code in Latest People Profile Header – SAP SuccessFactors EC Workaround
entry.content:
Hi @rushdarizvie ,

First, enable the "Show External Codes" option under Settings > People Profile Data Display. When this setting is enabled, external codes will be displayed in the latest People Profile only for users who have turned on this option. They will not be visible to all employees. I hope this works for you and helps avoid any confusion.

If you would like to display the external codes in a custom field, as suggested by @fvt , you can create a custom field in Job Information via Manage Business Configuration > Job Information. The field should be configured as read-only, and you can create an onView business rule to populate the data in the field with the required value (for example, Company/Position External Code).

Thanks,
Arfat

entry.title: Improved Data Access in Stories: Introducing the Data Panel and Drag & Drop Creation of Charts
entry.content:
The BDC SAP Analytics Cloud Team is constantly looking for ways to make data storytelling faster, more intuitive, and more accessible. With the 2026 Q2 QRC, we are excited to introduce a new foundation and set of improvements that fundamentally rethink how you discover, access, and work with data in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories.

 

The Data Panel: Your Starting Point for Self-Service Analytics

The Data Panel is a brand-new feature that builds on top of our Available Object List concept, which was introduced with the Optimized Experience. While the Available Object List was a first step, we recognized that it did not fulfill its potential as it always required you to select an existing object and launch via the Builder Panel. With this in mind, we've made significant improvements, not only to decouple the experience, but to meaningfully improve overall usability.

Transparent Data Access

In the past, consumers found it difficult to figure out how to add a data model to a Story. Most of the time, consumers would add an existing object which limited them to existing models or datasets, without realizing there was an additional entry point in the toolbar that provided access to data sources or to upload a file.

We are proud to announce that friction is now gone. Immediately when you create a new story, the Data Panel is surfaced right at the forefront, improving self-service workflows from the very first moment.

The Data Panel makes it immediately clear that you can add data from:

  • An Existing Model or Dataset

  • A Data Source

  • Upload a File

 

Recents & Favourites in Model Selection: Find Your Data Faster

We've recognized that consumers who consistently build insights from the same set of data models are frustrated by having to search for the same model again and again.

Now, once you select an existing model or dataset, you have access to:

  • Recents: data models you have actively used in the past, surfaced immediately so you can reuse them with a single click

  • Favourites: models you've explicitly starred for quick access

This dramatically reduces the number of clicks required to introduce a new data model into your story, especially for consumers who work repeatedly with the same datasets. Rather than browsing through folder structures or relying on search, your most relevant models are always a click away.

ⓘ it is an improvement that is available across SAP Analytics Cloud, such as Open a Theme, Save As a Story, and so on

 

Central Overview of Data

Once a data model has been added, the Data Panel becomes your central reference point for understanding and working with your data. Rather than navigating away from your Story, you can explore the full structure of the model directly within the panel:

  • Primary Structure (Account or Measure as it depends on your model)

  • Secondary Structure (Measure or Structure as it depends on your model)

  • Dimensions

  • Location Dimensions

This makes it significantly easier for consumers to understand the overall structure of their data model as they build additional visualizations, all without leaving the story canvas.

 

Continuous Exploration Across Data Models

It was very important to also enable continuous exploration. Consumers can now add additional data models directly within the Data Panel, and as each new model is added it is surfaced immediately, making it easier to navigate across multiple data models within the same story.

For complex dashboards, the Data Panel goes even further. As a consumer selects a specific visualization on the canvas, the Data Panel dynamically updates to reflect the data model associated with that visualization. If the consumer clicks away, the panel retains the last used data model, ready for the next visualization to be created.

For Blended Visualizations, selecting the visualization will invoke the full 'All Data' workflow, clearly highlighting via a blue indicator which models are in use, so consumers always have clarity on the data sources driving their analysis.

More Usability Improvements!

We also addressed a number of usability requests that customers have raised in the past:

  • Toggle between Description, ID, and ID & Description in the Data Panel making it easy to switch how entities are displayed 
  • Resize the Data Panel to suit your screen and content needs
  • Open in Modeller or Data Analyzer: under the three-dot menu, consumers can now open the current data model in a new tab, either in the Modeller or the Data Analyzer for further exploration. 

Decoupled from the Builder Panel

One of the most significant changes is the decoupling of the Data Panel from the Builder Panel. In the past, the Available Object List was always invoked through the Builder Panel.

Now, a lightweight toolbar on the right has been introduced, making it easier to:

  • Access or collapse the Data Panel independently
  • Access the Styling Panel or Builder Panel directly, without having to first open the Designer Panel
  • Collapse or Expand all right panels at once with a single action

This separation means the Data Panel can stand on its own as the primary workspace for data-first workflows, with the Builder Panel available as a manual configuration tool rather than the default starting point.

Powerful Search with Number of Entities Preview

For stories with large or complex data models, finding the right entity quickly is essential. As soon as a consumer invokes search within the Data Panel, a preview of matching entities is shown in real time, something that was not possible before.

For more complex data models, such as Universal Account Models with Multiple Hierarchies, the search experience goes even further. Matching entities are shown not only in the main panel list, but also within the account hierarchy dropdown menus, ensuring consumers can locate the exact field they need regardless of where it sits in the model structure.

Drag & Drop for Charts: Build Visualizations Naturally

With the Data Panel now surfaced alongside your story canvas, we've also improved drag and drop chart creation. The combination of the two makes this capability significantly more powerful.

Consumers now have two supported drag and drop workflows:

  • Drag from the Data Panel into the Builder Panel: the existing workflow, still fully supported
  • Drag from the Data Panel directly onto a Chart Widget on the canvas

Because the Data Panel is now decoupled, consumers can collapse the Builder Panel entirely and continue to build and modify charts purely through drag and drop, reducing panel clutter and keeping the focus on the canvas.

Updating Existing Visualizations

Previously, changing the data model on an existing visualization required the consumer to first clear the data source manually, which was a multi-click process. Now, consumers can simply drag a field from a different data model in the Data Panel directly onto an existing chart or into the Builder Panel, and the visualization will dynamically update to the new data model.

Where relevant, a confirmation prompt will appear to let the consumer know that the drag and drop action will cause a reset of the current chart configuration, keeping consumers informed while still dramatically reducing the number of steps required.

 

Building the Foundation for Data-First Workflows

With the introduction of the Data Panel, we've built the foundation for more advanced self-service use cases. The goal is to reduce the dependency on the Builder Panel as the primary interface, and instead establish a data-first workflow where consumers start with their data, explore its structure, and build visualizations naturally. The Builder Panel remains available for manual configuration, but it is no longer the gatekeeping step it once was.

These improvements, the Data Panel, Recents & Favourites, and enhanced Drag & Drop, are not isolated changes. Together they represent a deliberate shift toward a more intuitive, flexible, and powerful authoring experience in SAP Analytics Cloud Stories.

We're looking forward to hearing your feedback as you explore these features in the 2026 Q2 QRC. Keep a lookout on the Roadmap Explorer to see when we extend this to Tables and allow Drag & Drop directly to the canvas!

entry.title: Mit Snap! Schule stärken SAP und die sparks4school Foundation die digitale Bildung an Schulen
entry.content:
Liebe Lehrkräfte und liebe interessierte SAP Community Mitglieder,

in einer zunehmend digital geprägten Welt stehen Lehrkräfte täglich vor der Aufgabe, Schülerinnen und Schüler auf eine Zukunft vorzubereiten, in der informatische Grundbildung unverzichtbar ist. Viele Schulen wünschen sich dafür Werkzeuge, die leicht zugänglich, pädagogisch durchdacht und ohne technische Hürden einsetzbar sind.

Genau hier setzt Snap! Schule an – ein gemeinsames Open-Source-Projekt von SAP und der sparks4school foundation.

Unser Ziel ist klar: Informatikbildung für alle ermöglichen – unabhängig von sozialem oder kulturellem Hintergrund und unabhängig vom individuellen Kenntnisstand der Lernenden.

Was bietet Ihnen Snap! Schule?

  • Browserbasierte Nutzung ohne Installation – inklusive fertiger Puzzles und Begleitmaterial
  • Keine Vorkenntnisse erforderlich: Lehrkräfte benötigen weder Informatikstudium noch Programmiererfahrung
  • Niedrigschwelliger Einstieg und gleichzeitig flexibel für anspruchsvollere Projekte

Die Plattform ist die erste schulische Lösung, die die visuelle Blockprogrammiersprache Snap! gezielt an deutsche Bildungspläne anpasst. Snap! wird seit Jahren an Schulen und Universitäten eingesetzt und eignet sich sowohl für erste Schritte in der Informatik als auch für komplexere Anwendungen.

Besonders für Lehrkräfte in Baden-Württemberg ist snap.schule relevant: Die Plattform ist speziell für den Unterricht in „Informatik und Medienbildung“ ab Klasse 6 konzipiert und wird gemeinsam mit Partnern aus Hochschulen, Schulen und Industrie kontinuierlich weiterentwickelt. 

 

Interesse geweckt? Alle Infos und die Anmeldung zu unseren Webinaren finden Sie hier:

🌐snap.schule
📅events.sparks4school.org/snap

💬Haben Sie Fragen? Hinterlassen Sie gerne einen Kommentar unter diesem Beitrag – wir antworten Ihnen gerne!

entry.title: Variable currency element in Simple Transformation
entry.content:
Hi Folks,

I'm working on a Simple Transformation in XSLT_TOOL to  convert data from ABAP to XML.  It works fine,  but there is one improvement I need to make. 

The data comes from an SD invoice,  and each value element needs to be accompanied by a currency element.  And the currency has to appear on the same line in the XML as the value.

So I need an XML output line that looks something like this:

<Amount currencyID="EUR">100.00</Amount>

Thus far,  the relevant line in my ST looks like this:

<Amount currencyID="EUR" tt:value-ref=".INVOICE.AMOUNT"/>

Clearly, the currency here is a literal;  but I need to insert it as a variable instead.  It's in my ABAP structure as INVOICE.CURRENCY.

So how do I include the currency variable, without it appearing on a separate line?

Don't think I haven't searched for this;  everything I've found so far either outputs 2 lines instead of 1,  or doesn't compile (thank you AI).  I'm sure the answer is trivial...

Thanks for reading,

John

entry.title: Rethinking Transformation: Why Procurement Is Leading the Next ERP Evolution 🚀
entry.content:
Rethinking Transformation: Why Procurement Is Leading the Next ERP Evolution 🚀

Digital transformation has become a familiar term, but for many organizations it still feels abstract, risky, or overwhelming. In a recent episode of The Future of Procurement, Curt Reppart, Associate Partner at IBM, offered a grounded and experience-driven perspective on how companies can modernize procurement and ERP landscapes without losing momentum—or value—along the way. 💡

Drawing on more than a decade in SAP consulting and procurement transformation, Reppart framed a compelling message: successful transformation is less about technology choices and more about behavior, data, and intentional change.

Beyond Greenfield and Brownfield: The Rise of Selective Transformation 🔄

One of the most valuable insights from the discussion was the clear explanation of ERP transformation paths. Traditionally, organizations have faced a binary choice: brownfield (lift-and-shift the existing system as-is) or greenfield (start fresh with standard processes). While each has merits, both extremes come with trade-offs—either carrying forward technical debt or absorbing massive disruption. ⚖️

Reppart highlighted the growing adoption of selective transformation, often referred to as “bluefield.” This approach blends technical conversion with deliberate business decisions about what data, processes, and configurations to keep, redesign, or retire. The result is a more pragmatic migration to S/4HANA that balances speed, cost, and long-term flexibility. 🧭

Procurement as the Ideal Transformation Entry Point 🛒

Why does procurement consistently emerge as a strong candidate for transformation? According to Reppart, procurement processes should be inherently standardized across industries, yet they are often among the most over-customized areas in legacy ERP environments. 🧩

Years of workarounds, exception handling, and disconnected supplier communication have created unnecessary complexity. Modern procurement platforms—particularly those leveraging supplier networks—offer an opportunity to simplify internal workflows while dramatically improving external collaboration. 🌐

Reppart emphasized that true transformation extends beyond the four walls of the enterprise. Supplier enablement, compliance, and real-time collaboration are no longer “nice-to-haves”; they are foundational to resilience and scalability.

Transformation Is About People, Not Just Platforms 👥

A recurring theme throughout the conversation was the importance of changing behaviors, not just systems. New technology alone will not fix broken processes. In fact, without behavioral change, organizations often recreate old problems on new platforms. 🔁

Reppart stressed the need to engage stakeholders at multiple levels—from the C-suite defining outcomes, to mid-level leaders owning transformation decisions, to frontline users who understand how work truly gets done. Listening to those closest to the process often reveals hidden dependencies and manual steps that formal documentation overlooks. 🎧

This grassroots insight is critical in determining which processes are stable, which are held together by “band-aids,” and where transformation will deliver the greatest value.

Data as the Foundation for AI-Driven Procurement 🤖

Looking ahead, Reppart offered a pragmatic take on artificial intelligence in procurement. While AI is now part of every executive conversation, its success depends entirely on data quality and information architecture(IA). “You can’t have AI without IA,” he noted. 📊

As AI agents and automation capabilities mature, procurement professionals will rely less on deep system navigation skills and more on the ability to ask the right questions—effectively becoming prompt-driven operators. Over time, interfaces may matter less than outcomes, with users interacting through a single conversational layer that orchestrates multiple intelligent agents behind the scenes. 🧠

The Road Ahead: Progress Over Perfection 🛤️

Reppart’s closing perspective was clear: transformation is not about being first or perfect, but about being intentional. Organizations that invest early in clean core principles, strong data models, and scalable processes will be better positioned to adopt future innovations—whether AI-driven or otherwise. ✅

Procurement, with its direct impact on cost, risk, and supplier relationships, is uniquely positioned to lead this journey. And as Reppart underscored, modernization isn’t about starting over—it’s about knowing what to take with you. 🔍

Apple: Listen on Apple!

Spotify: Listen on Spotify!

entry.title: Re: How to configure business goals that can change in time
entry.content:
You are missing the key aspect of the answer.  It seems the employee can only 2 goals at any one time.  If they change goal class, they should move from one plan to another.   You need to have a plan for each combination.  The 30/30 plan needs to have the goals = 100% so it is really a 50/50 plan.   Similarly, the 15/45 plan, is 25/75, etc.  

So, if the employee changes from the 30/30 plan to the 15/45 plan on June 1, the history file would look something like this:

UserID     Start      End             Goal Class    Salary     Target     Basis

123456   1/1/26   5/31/26     30/30            50000      10           5000

123456   6/1/26   12/31/26   15/45            60000      15           9000

In summary, you need multiple plans, not 1 plan that is searching for the right version of multiple goals.

entry.title: Re: crystal reports compatibility
entry.content:
Firstly don't mix runtime versions, it causes all sorts of problems for you.

Go here and get the latest SP 40 for CR for VS, note there are separate installers into VS for VS 2010->2019 and for 64 bit versions of VS 2022 and above.

Use the EXE to integrate into VS, MSI are installed so you don't need to install those on your DEV PC, for deploying only.

CR SP 40 now only supports 64 bit applications.

You can install the 32 bit EXE for VS 2019.

To see all the latest info go here:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/crystalreports/3354091173.html?locale=en-US&ai=true

See my blog on upgrading:

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/upgrading-a-visual-studio-2008-gt-2022-net-project-with-the-latest-cr-net/ba-p/13469215

Highly recommend upgrade CR Designer also, CR 8.5 is very old so most of it's dependencies are no longer supported by both SAP and Microsoft. You can get CR 2025 from here:

https://www.sap.com/products/data-cloud/crystal-reports.html

entry.title: Re: Restricted KYF in Datasphere using hierarchy nodes wi...
entry.content:
Hey Tomas,

there is no standard feature yet. The dsp roadmap for Q3 2026 shows an item that meets your requirement:

"Restricted measure based on node-level hierarchy

Overview
Analytic model allowing definition of restricted measures that restrict on a node of a hierarchy, with this node either being fixed or set by the user using a prompt
Ability for the children of the node to consequently be included in the restriction

Benefits
Enable more expressive and versatile measure definitions. Feature parity with SAP Business Warehouse."

Until then, you could define this structure in a sac story or you build your own derivation logic in dsp, for instance via restricted key figure variables and lookup views.

Kind regards,

Tano

entry.title: Re: Lessons Learned: Fetching Learning History from SuccessFactors LMS Using OData APIs
entry.content:
Hi Aayush, thanks for posting this.

I just want to point out that in the API guide, in the "Most Preferred Services" section (page 7 of the 1H 2026 version of the PDF, but I remember seeing it in previous versions as well), it says that you should try whenever possible to use endpoints with *-service

So, for your example, instead of 

/learning/odatav4/public/user/learningHistory/v1/learninghistorys?
$filter=criteria/targetUserID eq 'STUDENT001'

the preferred endpoint would be:

/learning/odatav4/public/user/userlearning-service/v1/LearningHistories?
actingAs=admin&$filter=criteria/targetUserID eq 'STUDENT001'

Today I participated in Business Builders, a hands-on experience focused on data analysis for decision-making using SAP tools. The session was led by Prof. Sergio De La Cruz, an innovation leader in SAP simulation-based teaching and international professor at HEC Montréal, and was made possible by DAProd — the Academic Council of the Production Engineering program.

During the activity, we interacted with SAP Analytics Cloud dashboards to answer questions and solve challenges based on real business scenarios. The experience also demonstrated, in practice, how Business Intelligence tools and the strategic organization of data directly impact decision-making within companies.

I am leaving this session with a new perspective on the value of data organization and how BI tools expand analytical capabilities far beyond what Excel allows. The gamified format also stood out for its effectiveness — learning by simulating real scenarios significantly accelerates content absorption.

#SAP #BusinessBuilders #DataAnalysis #HECMontreal

entry.title: Production Connector as MQTT client: New connection settings and Last Will & Testament messages
entry.content:
Production Connector as MQTT client: New connection settings and Last Will & Testament messages

With the 2605 release of SAP Digital Manufacturing, the Production Connector introduces enhanced MQTT client capabilities that improve reliability, observability, and connection handling. This blog explains the new configuration settings—Last Will and Testament (LWT), Clean Session, and Keep Alive—and demonstrates how they enable robust communication scenarios and help detect ungraceful disconnects and notify subscribed systems in real time.

Introduction

In modern manufacturing integration scenarios, reliable messaging is critical—especially when leveraging MQTT for lightweight, event-driven communication. With the 2605 release of SAP Digital Manufacturing and Production Connector 3.1, the Production Connector evolves further as an MQTT client by introducing three new configuration capabilities:

  • Last Will and Testament (LWT)
  • Keep Alive Interval
  • Clean Session

These settings align the Production Connector more closely with standard MQTT behavior and significantly enhance resiliency in distributed environments.

Understanding the new MQTT connection settings

1- Last Will and Testament (LWT)

The Last Will and Testament is a built-in MQTT safety mechanism. It allows a client to register a message with the broker during connection setup. If the client disconnects unexpectedly (without sending a proper disconnect signal), the broker automatically publishes this message to a predefined topic:

 

Key configuration elements:

  • Topic: Where the LWT message will be published
  • Payload: The content of the message
  • Quality of Service (QoS): Delivery guarantee level
  • Retain flag: Keeps the last known state available for new subscribers

This mechanism is especially useful for monitoring scenarios:

When the Production Connector goes offline ungracefully, all clients subscribed to the configured topic are immediately notified.


2 - Keep Alive Interval

The Keep Alive setting acts as a heartbeat between client and broker:

  • Defines how frequently the client must send a ping
  • If no ping is received within the interval, the broker assumes the client is disconnected
  • The broker triggers the LWT message if the disconnect is ungraceful

In the Production Connector the default value is 30 seconds. Depending on local network conditions the customer may have the need to change to higher values to reduce network traffic.

This mechanism ensures:

  • Fast detection of connection loss
  • Immediate notification through LWT


3- Clean Session

The Clean Session flag determines whether the broker should persist session data across reconnects.

  • Clean Session = true
    • Session data is discarded after disconnect
    • No subscriptions or queued messages are retained
    • Each connection starts fresh

  • Clean Session = false
    • Session state is preserved
    • Subscriptions and queued QoS 1 and 2 messages are retained
    • Ensures continuity in unstable networks

For production environments, setting this to false is typically recommended to guarantee reliable communication despite intermittent connectivity. Only for scenarios with lightweight brokers the flag may be set to "true".

 

Configuration Steps

Within the Manage Message Broker app, new configuration sections are available for:

  • Subscriber client
  • Publisher client

The subscriber client uses a dedicated agent instance and maintains a single connection to the broker. Only the subscriber client has a “Clean Session” flag while the LWT and Keep Alive settings are available for all clients.

Publisher clients are created dynamically through automation sequences: Each MQTT publish step creates its own client. Subscriptions in the Manage Automatic Triggers app also create clients, if “Message Publishing” is selected as action type.

This means:

  • Multiple publisher clients can exist simultaneously
  • Currently, they share the same configuration settings

In the example shown below we have configured for the subscriber client:

  • An LWT payload was defined:
    "Production connector subscriber client went offline"
  • A dedicated topic was configured, here lwtTopic/subscriber
  • For the Keep Alive interval we kept its default value of 30 seconds
  • We are using the default Quality of Service 0 – At Most Once

Conclusion

With the introduction of LWT, Keep Alive, and Clean Session settings, the Production Connector becomes a more robust and transparent MQTT client. These capabilities empower developers and operators to:

  • Detect and react to failures instantly
  • Ensure message delivery continuity
  • Build more reliable industrial integration scenarios

As MQTT adoption continues to grow in manufacturing, these enhancements are a significant step toward production-grade messaging reliability in SAP Digital Manufacturing.

If you’re working with MQTT integration in Production Connector, now is a great time to explore these settings and integrate them into your architecture.

entry.title: SAP BTP ABAP Environment – How to Correct the System Type
entry.content:
What are System Types?

SAP BTP ABAP Environment (a.k.a. Steampunk) distinguishes two types of systems. The system type is set via parameter is_development_allowed (true or false) during system creation. The default is true (see documentation). Until now, the parameter value cannot be changed for an existing system. The two system types are:

  • development system - parameter is_development_allowed = true
  • non-development system - parameter is_development_allowed = false

The main characteristics are:

CharacteristicsDevelopmentNon-Development
System flavoursdevelopment, sandboxtest, production
Manual changes of development objects are allowed in ADTyesno (objects can only be changed via SWC clone/pull or add-on deployment)
Developer role SAP_BR_DEVELOPER does existyesno
Downtime-optimized update and upgrade appliednoyes

 

Why a Correction Might be Needed?

As the default during system creation is development system, the setting is sometimes not considered as important and not changed properly for test and production system. A typical mistake is, that test and productive systems are setup as development system. An incorrect setup has a significant impact as explained in the table above. Therefore, a correct setup is highly recommended, especially to benefit from the downtime-optimized updates and upgrades (see blog post).

 

How to Correct the System Type?

Prerequistes

Mandatory manual adjustments:

  1. Make sure that there is no open transport.
  2. Make sure that no additional test tenant resides on the system. This system type switch is currently not supported if you have created a test tenant via the Landscape Portal.
  3. Remove all business roles which contain the business catalog SAP_A4C_BC_DEV_PC for development. One of these business roles is the generated role SAP_BR_DEVELOPER. Please remove SAP_BR_DEVELOPER. But there might be also additional custom-defined business roles which need to be adjusted, for example, by removing the business catalog SAP_A4C_BC_DEV_PC from the role definition.

Perform the Correction

For the time being, a service request has to be filed via support ticket on BC-CP-ABA containing the following information:

  • Title: Request for system type switch for SAP BTP ABAP Environment system
  • Description: Please switch <instance id> to a non-development system
  • Priority: Medium

You can find the <instance id> of the respective system either in the SAP BTP Cockpit or in the properties of the respective ABAP Cloud project in the ABAP Development Tools (ADT) as first part of the system URL. Please notice, that the 3-character system id is not necessarily unique in your landscape and therefore not sufficent. 

A downtime of approx. 5 minutes is required for the actual system conversion which will happen during Walldorf business hours.

Validate the Correction

Once the ticket has been processed and your system has been converted, you will find the following inherent changes:

  1. The SAP Fiori Launchpad About dialog show the system role Production System and the tenant role Production Tenant.
  2. The business role template SAP_BR_DEVELOPER is no longer available in the Maintain Business Roles app.

entry.title: Communicating the value of SAP Business Data Cloud using personas
entry.content:
Across many partner conversations, a common theme appears: data is split across tools, teams, and systems, which slows decisions and adds effort.

This webinar for SAP Partners focuses on how to lead clearer customer discussions around SAP Business Data Cloud (SAP BDC) using persona-led talk tracks.

You will explore how Data Engineers, Data Scientists, and Business Analysts each define value differently, and how SAP BDC supports them through a unified data layer, governed semantic layer, and zero-copy access across SAP and non-SAP sources.

We will connect real customer pain points such as slow reporting and inconsistent data to capabilities like curated data products, real-time insights, and integration between SAP Datasphere and SAP Databricks in a broader data ecosystem.

You will also practice discovery questions that guide conversations toward outcomes linked to total cost of ownership and faster decision cycles.

Register now  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/dec/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00012384.html

SAP SAPBTP SAPPartners SAPEcosystem SAPBusinessDataCloud SAPDatasphere SAPDatabricks

entry.title: Hands-on Tutorial: Machine Learning with SAP HANA Cloud and BAS
entry.content:
Machine Learning on structured data might not be what many people have in mind when talking about AI. Yet, it is a form of AI and the massive interest in Large Language Models, agents and assistants is also leading to many new Machine Learning Projects. 

With this blog you can get hands-on with Machine Learning by implementing a time-series forecast in SAP HANA Cloud. In the requirements and projects that I am seeing, time-series forecasting is by far the most popular Machine Learning approach in a SAP related project. From Accounts Receivable forecasting, to demand forecasting and even solar power prediction, a time-series forecast might get you there.

IMPORTANT: You can download all code from this repository.

 

Architecture

The architecture for this example is as straightforward as it can be. Your data is in SAP HANA Cloud, where we also have Machine Learning algorithms (Predictive Analysis Library and Automated Predictive Library). We then use Python in a Jupyter Notebook running in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger the Machine Learning algorithms in SAP HANA Cloud. This is possible with SAP's hana_ml Python package. You can also choose any other Python environment of your choice to use that package to trigger the built-in algorithms. No data ever has to leave SAP HANA Cloud, since data and ML algorithm are already in the same place.

This blog focuses mostly on a stand-alone SAP HANA Cloud. However, you can also trigger the same Machine Learning in SAP Datasphere's embedded SAP HANA Cloud. This blog has more details. 

More components are typically needed for a productive environment. To automate the creation of predictions the Python code can be schedules for instance with Cloud Foundry or SAP AI Core. And the time-series algorithm we are using is just one of 100+ algorithms for you to choose from. Here is an overview. See the hana_ml documentation for details.

 

Prerequisites

This blog assumes that:

  • You have SAP Business Application Studio up and running and configured as described in "Setting up Python in SAP Business Application Studio to trigger HANA Cloud Machine Learning"
  • You have access to a SAP HANA Cloud instance, that is configured as described in the same above blog.

 

Data Load

You will use historic data from the Swiss tourism area to predict how many nights people will spend in a hotel each month in the next 12 months. Download the file HOTELNIGHTS.csv and import it into SAP HANA Cloud, for instance with the SAP HANA Database Explorer. Select "Import Data".

 

Click through the options:

  • name the new table: HOTELNIGHTS
  • set the following column data types
    • MONTH: Date
    • ARRIVALS: Integer
    • HOTELNIGHTS: Integer

 

You will see that the information in that table is quite detailed. It shows for the period from January 2020 to March 2026 by month how many people from which Country spent how many nights in a hotel in the various regions of Switzerland.

 

Time-series forecast

Now use the data for a time-series forecast! Script in Python and the hana_ml package translates your logic into SQL and has SAP HANA Cloud do the work. 

IMPORTANT: You can download the notebook HANAML time series forecast.ipynb, which has the following code included. If you upload that file into your environment and execute the first cell, you will be prompted to select the Python environment. This can be easily overlooked...

If you are meeting the prerequisites (see above), you should already be able to connect form a Jupyter notebook in BAS to connect to your SAP HANA Cloud. Continue now by storing the database user's details a in a credentials file.

{
  "SAP_HANA_CLOUD": {
    "HANA_ADDRESS": "YOURENDPOINT",
    "HANA_PORT": 443,
    "HANA_USER": "AIUSER",
    "HANA_PASSWORD": "YOURSECRETPASSWORD"
  }
}

 

Load these credentials in Python and verify that the connection still returns True.

import json
with open('./credentials.json', 'r') as creds:
  credentials = json.load(creds)
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS  = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_ADDRESS"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PORT"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER     = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_USER"]
SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD = credentials["SAP_HANA_CLOUD"]["HANA_PASSWORD"]
import hana_ml.dataframe as dataframe
conn = dataframe.ConnectionContext(address  = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_ADDRESS,
                                   port     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PORT,
                                   user     = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_USER,
                                   password = SAP_HANA_CLOUD_PASSWORD)
conn.connection.isconnected()

In case you see a compatibility warning for Python 3.0.2, feel free to ignore it.

Continue by creating a hana_ml DataFrame, which points to the Data in SAP HANA Cloud. The data remains in SAP HANA Cloud. No records have yet left the system. This is by default and on purpose. The intention is to keep the data in the system and do any data processing within SAP HANA Cloud.

data_hdf = conn.table('HOTELNIGHTS')

 

It is possible to retrieve data though. You just need to request it with the collect()-method, which downloads the data of the hana_ml DataFrame as Pandas DataFrame. To avoid downloading all records, first restrict the data to 5 rows with the head()-method. The sequence is very important here. First reduce the number of records, then download.

data_hdf.head(5).collect()

 

To get a more detailed feel for the data, have some statistics calculated.

data_hdf.describe().collect()

 

There are 13 Swiss regions in the data for example and the maximum number of hotel nights in a specific months in a specific region from a specific country is 223091. Important here is the fact that every value in that table was calculated in SAP HANA Cloud. Imagine you are working with a massive table, you will want to leverage the processing power of SAP HANA Cloud. That's ensured here, since the hana_ml package created the necessary SQL statement. That SQL syntax can be seen by adding ".select_statement" to the command. The screenshot shows only a fraction of the SQL.

import pprint
pprint.pp(data_hdf.describe().select_statement)

 

One of many other ways to explore the data is an interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(data_hdf).build().display()

 

We are happy with the data and need to prepare it now for a monthly forecast of all hotel nights in the country. Aggregate by month. 

agg_hdf = data_hdf.agg([('sum', 'HOTELNIGHTS', 'HOTELNIGHTS')], group_by='MONTH')
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.sort('MONTH')
agg_hdf.head(5).collect()

As usual, that aggregation is also done on SAP HANA Cloud and only 5 aggregated values are downloaded. Also as usual, you could add ".select_statement" to see the exact SQL syntax that is used.

Plot the monthly data to get a visual impression for the data. This requires the installation of another Python package. Run this cell once. After the package is installed:

  • you may want to delete the cell (or comment it out) to avoid unnecessary reinstallation attempts)
  • and you may need to restart the Python kernel so that the new package can be used. Click the "Restart" icon in the menu on top and run the earlier cells again.
!pip install nbformat # Once installed, turn this line into a comment to avoid running it again

 

Download the monthly aggregates and create a line chart.

import plotly.express as px
data_df = agg_hdf.collect()
fig =  px.line(data_df, x='MONTH', y='HOTELNIGHTS', title='Hotel nights by Month')
fig.show()

There is a beautiful repeating pattern in the data. True seasonality. Peaks in the summer, lows in November and lower peaks in winter. Train a time-series model! We use the AdditiveModelForecast which is an implementation of Meta's Prophet algorithm.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.tsa.additive_model_forecast import AdditiveModelForecast
amf = AdditiveModelForecast()
amf.fit(data=agg_hdf)

 

Explore the trained model in another interactive standard report.

from hana_ml.visualizers.unified_report import UnifiedReport
UnifiedReport(amf).build().display()

 

With that trained model we can forecast the unknown future. Just prepare a hana_ml DataFrame that contains the months (or dates), for which we want forecasts. In a productive scenario you will probably connect to a table that's permanently updated with new records. Hence, to keep the code dynamic for scheduling, find out from the data the most recent month for which data is available. 

str_lastdate = agg_hdf.tail(1, ref_col='MONTH').collect().iloc[0,0]
str_lastdate = str(str_lastdate)[0:10]
print(str_lastdate)

 

Use that month as basis create the required dates for the forecast. We need 12 records, always the first day of the following 12 months.

from hana_ml.algorithms.pal.random import binomial
months_to_forecast=12
future_hdf = binomial(conn, n=1, p=1, num_random=months_to_forecast)
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('*', (f'''ADD_MONTHS(TO_DATE ('{str_lastdate}', 'YYYY-MM-DD'), ID+1)''', 'MONTH') )
future_hdf = future_hdf.select('MONTH', ('0', 'TARGET'))
future_hdf.head(20).collect()

 

Apply the trained model to predict those dates. And you get the prediction (YHAT) as well as the prediction interval (YHAT_LOWER and YHAT_UPPER).

predicted_hdf = amf.predict(data=future_hdf)
predicted_hdf.head(20).collect()

Before using the predictions, have a closer look at the trained model and the forecasted values in the standard report.

 

The outlier tab points out a few months in 2020 that are unusual. Indeed, there was something that year that impacted travelling and hotel nights....  Cutting off that old history would be a good idea. You could go back to the top of the Notebook and add a filter to the data, ie 

data_hdf = data_hdf.filter("YEAR(MONTH) >= 2022")

 

In this tutorial we continue with the model as it is. You could save the predictions just as they are. However, you may also want to combine the past actuals with the predictions into a single table since that might make it easier to put a dashboard on top for instance.

predicted_hdf = predicted_hdf.select('MONTH',
                                    ('NULL', 'HOTELNIGHTS'),
                                    ('YHAT', 'FORECAST'),
                                    ('YHAT_LOWER', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                                    ('YHAT_UPPER', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
agg_hdf = agg_hdf.select('*', 
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_LOWER'),
                        ('NULL', 'FORECAST_UPPER'))
actualsandpred_hdf = predicted_hdf.union(agg_hdf)
actualsandpred_hdf.sort('MONTH').tail(5).collect()

 

Finally, save that combined data into a table in SAP HANA Cloud. In case that table doesn't exist yet, it will be automatically created in the format of the hana_ml DataFrame.

actualsandpred_hdf.save('HOTELNIGHTS_FORECAST', force=True)

 

With this you have enriched your data in SAP HANA Cloud through Machine Learning! The actuals in the HOTELNIGHTS column end in March 2026 with the FORECAST continuing on from April 2026 to March 2027.

 

Related topics

With such a forecast you can already achieve a lot. You may also want to extend it further or use a completely different algorithm. These resources can help you along, but feel free to ask if you have any questions!

  • Documentation hana_ml package
  • Documentation of the underlying Predictive Analysis Library
  • Sample code for the Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) and also the Automated Predictive Library (APL)

entry.title: Re: What is the OData API timeout on S/4Hana Clould
entry.content:
Hello, 

Since this information is not available on the documentation, the effective inbound API processing timeout in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenant is:

  • SOAP: 600 seconds (10 minutes)
  • OData V4: 600 seconds (10 minutes)
  • REST/HTTP APIs: 600 seconds (10 minutes).

entry.title: Can AI Agent replace IT Architects (EA/SA)?
entry.content:
Can AI (Artificial Intelligence) replace human intelligence? In theory or practice, probably any intelligence can be replaced with compatible intelligence capable of doing the same or better reasoning and decisions. But how close are we in AI Agents or Agentic AI to replace not only coding and programming, but also architectural thinking?

There is a perception – IT Architecture (EA or SA does not matter) is reasoning and decision process based on specific rules/guiderails, frameworks/guidelines – so why not putting all this into RAG (and well-designed vector DB) and let LLM does all the magic?

After all, why are we doing this, and do we see the potential consequences?

EA/SA is not (only) about frameworks

I was talking about this in past… Being an IT Architect is not about knowledge only – yes, it’s needed (it helps us “steer” – and much more), but nobody knows it all (and this is exactly what an IT Architect needs to embrace). Being an IT Architect is not even about having (only) great interpersonal skills and “character” – yeah for it is very helpful on many occasions, but there are some of us who are not always full or “understanding” and “compassion”…

IT Architecture is about overall mindset – mindset of “make it happen”!

Sometimes it’s strategy, roadmap or initiative – epic … But sometimes it can be even simple feature or user-story – just finding a solution to a problem… No, do not run away from solutioning because, we just “do strategy”! Strategy which is disconnected with pragmatic solutioning might not be a good strategy, after all? If we cannot translate (per need) this “strategy” into “solution”, how can we except others will?

It’s all about “make it happen” – IT Architecture is “strategic” discipline, but it has to be “grounded” to address actual problems and needs. No more “ivory towers” – please!

Sometimes I am just an engineer, sometimes I am an advisor, sometimes I am a leader (“leading” cross platform teams), or sometimes I do solution design… And yes, sometimes I get my hands “dirty” as well – why not… Yet all the time, I am an IT Architect... How is this possible? Because IT Architecture is not simple discipline which can be easily boxed in the bunch of rules. Or it may seem like that?

Why AI to replace us?

Stop selling me the idea “AI is here (only) to help us” – sorry this is just a marketing. Yes, it might be the price of the progress, but don’t tell me “we build AI because you need it”… No, I was doing my EA/SA work just fine without AI, and I do not ask to be “replaced”! Well, this is at least what I say... 

But I do not stop here – I have more controversial things to say…

People working on Manhattan Project produced what they have produced – and don’t tell me the motivation was to produce something “good” (or at least it did not turn out to be something “good” – many would say)…

  • They did it (mostly) because they were “intrigued” to make scientific break-through. It was a challenge, a “puzzle” which needed to be “solved”. This is understandable – as this is what scientists do…
  • They did it (mostly) without having second-thoughts about possible consequences. Now, it does not mean one is good or bad because of this – it just means, not enough consideration for the future use was given at the time…
  • And yes, the circumstances of the historical moment did influence some (many) decisions – we cannot neglect this…
  • And finally, yes there are good (peaceful) applications for all the achievements accomplished. So, things are never “black and white” – we must be fair and say this openly, no matter what the direct association on the project itself might be … 

One may say it is inappropriate to compare all this with AI “hype”. I am being “way over” too harsh to AI? Well, let’s think a bit more!

AI evolution or something different…

AI (r)evolution is not the same as invention of the wheel, or the pump, or even gunpowder… It’s not the same as the industrial revolution either, even though there are some similarities. Yes, AI tools can be very helpful, but overall, AI concept can become very (globally) destructive as well. Are we (really) taking into consideration all the potential consequences of AI taking over reasoning, decisions and actions from us – in various fields? The problem is, we are building something we might not fully understand – something without a “plug” to turn on/off (as opposing to "machines" from the the industrial revolution) – and we are handing over our (full?) control to this “something”. 

Remember: “A computer can never be held accountable, therefore a computer must never make a management decision.” – IBM Training Manual, 1979[1][2]. So why are we not respecting this?

Are we just pushing the limits with AI, until we ultimately produce Conscious AI (or Self-Aware AI)? Do we ask ourselves the questions “why”?

… So, is this what I say really too controversial?

Too strong…

This might sound like too strong view, or perhaps too dark view? 

Do not take me wrong, I am not against AI Agents or Agentic AI – indeed it can be very useful (in support of IT Architecture as well – why not), but has to be properly designed, built, managed, controlled and audited. The key word here is “governance”… And do we trust we will do “governance” right… This is the question, we need to answer! If we hesitate with an answer – maybe we need to think twice before rushing into AI “progress”. Maybe, just maybe, we need to set the guardrails first, and then start our way forward into AI “journey”?

So, this is where this “inappropriate” comparing comes from – let us now (at least) try to set the guardrails first, let us learn from the past! 

Why should AI not replace us?

Am I rising this voice because I am "frighten" IT Architects will be replaced by AI? Look, more-or-less anyone could potentially be replaced by some form of AI (if we look broader) – this is just a fact. 

I raise this voice, as I believe IT Architects are responsible to see things holistically and understand potential consequences better than others in the industry… Aren't this exactly what we normally do? 

And if we are to be replaced by AI, who will then try to bring this AI "guardrails" and AI "governance" in place? 

I am sure, some answers can be provided on this – but would those be good ones?  

---

Disclaimer: When I talk about AI, I express only my personal view. I am neither against nor pro AI – I am very much in favor of scientific and engineering progress, but I also call for a caution and proper regulations.

Acknowledgment

*) Intro photo generated by GenAI (Microsoft Copilot)

References

[1] IBM: AI decision-making: Where do businesses draw the line? | IBM

[2] LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7271085510142439426/

entry.title: Stay Certified Process Clarification and advice for all current SAP FI and CO certifications
entry.content:
Are you ready to renew your certification validity through the Stay Current process for your Private or Public Cloud certification in Financial or Management accounting in SAP S/4HANA? Then, read on to understand how to prepare and ace your Stay Certified assessment!

There is a single place you can go to learn all you need to learn, our learning journey Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance:

 

After going through the learning journey, you can take the Stay Certified exam through the Manage Certification option in your profile on the learning.sap.com platform:

 

A quick reminder that the Stay Current assessments consist of 8 to 12 questions, are non-proctored and you can repeat the tests as many times as needed to pass. This means there is no risk in attempting to pass after going through the materials.

A couple further notes to save you from possible frustration: 

  • If you hold multiple certifications, you need to renew their validity by taking the (same) test for each of them. Sorry for this inconvenience as it is technically not possible to cross check the separate assessments currently.
  • There is a standalone assessment associated with the learning journey Applying Business AI Solutions and Expertise in SAP Finance. This is (unfortunately) independent to the stay certified process. So, though the standalone assessment that leads to the knowledge badge is "harder" there is again no impact on the stay certified process and you will need to access and pass the assessment in any case.

    Good Luck with your assessments!

Dear Customer,

Thank you for reaching out to SAP Support, I reviewed your case description and the link from the Community. As per our process all WFA on HANA implementation issue should be addressed by the Expert this including issue such as "Question/Issue related to Key Mapping"

2850225 - WFA on HANA - Implementation query & Handling process
https://me.sap.com/notes/2850225/E

Since the issue is implementation issue related to Key Mapping", I will suggest that you follow up with  to the team  HCM Partner Delivery Group (PDG) for further assistance.

Note that Reporting support and WFA engineering will only assist with Functional issue such as: Functional Queries

If you are a partner/customer and has some issues about functional topics in WFA on HANA, feel free to raise an case to Cloud Support. Some examples of functional queries:

  • Navigating in WFA portal

entry.title: Re: New SAP Community - Profile Signatures
entry.content: There seems to be recent changes in the Community API which is causing this.  We can no longer query user information via the API. I'm making some changes to this tool, but now we will only be able to lookup user information for someone that has authored at least one post on the Community.

entry.title: unable get email to set up password after i subscribed cloud identity services on SAP BTP
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am using an SAP BTP Trial account and have successfully subscribed to Cloud Identity Services. But I am not getting email to set up my password.

I can log in to the SAP BTP Trial Cockpit using my registered email address without any issues. However, when I click "Go to Application" for Cloud Identity Services, I am redirected to the sign-in page and receive the error:

"Sorry, we couldn't authenticate you."

I have already tried the following:

  • Resetting my password in the Identity Authentication tenant

  • Logging out and logging back in

  • Using the same email address that works for the BTP Trial Cockpit

Thanks,

Gayathri.

entry.title: Re: Blog#1: Finally it is here - How "pony" brings custom fields to SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Hi @PClaus ,

great to see what you've achievied:

  • Custom Tile in the SAP Cloud ALM Launchpad
  • Extending SAP CALM Objects with Custom Fields
  • Integrating this into the SAP CALM Standard Search

I've did a thorrow research but can't find any pointer in the SAP Documentation for CALM that describes how that is possible. Can you explain what official way you've used to achieve that? If it is not any official API how will you ensure that your extension works when SAP decides to change anything?

Best regards
Gregor

entry.title: Re: MCP Server for SAP ECC & S/4HANA: Unlimited ABAP Add-On for Display, Create and Update Tools
entry.content:
1. Is the MCP server private cloud hosted? This is an AD&D sector requirement. The MCP server is 100% hosted directly within your ABAP system. We deliver it as an ABAP Add-on, which is installed and uninstalled via the standard SAP SAINT transaction.

2. Can you provide the ROI on Side-by-Side Joule vs. Joule + Skybuffer MCP? The Skybuffer MCP does not actually require SAP Joule. Our MCP server is designed to be consumed by any reasoning layer (for example, Anthropic, Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT, etc.).

Additionally, our complete Skybuffer AI platform (which provides the orchestration, reasoning, and complex tool layers) can also be deployed entirely on-premises and integrates seamlessly with self-hosted LLMs. This provides a truly fortified edition specifically designed for isolated and highly secure environments.

3. What is the rough cost for the MCP server usage? We operate on a predictable, fixed annual subscription model. You can find our transparent pricing for both the standalone MCP server and the full Skybuffer AI platform (which includes the MCP server) directly on the SAP Store.

4. How do we get updates? MCP server updates are provided as standard SAR files (ABAP add-on). Skybuffer AI platform updates are provided as MTAR files, supporting deployment on either the SAP HANA Platform (on-premise) or SAP BTP.

I would be happy to connect with you to discuss Skybuffer AI in more detail and show you a live demo of the platform in action. Please let me know if you have some time for a brief call!

entry.content:
Hi 

@JohannD Thanks ! 

@CarsonWongIVC If anyone is able to show how I can improve this design to do IC to PM directly without the reports and MDF import, I'd be more than happy to edit my blog. I don't think it's possible based on my research due to limitations on IC side (FYI, CPI experts looked at this requirement I solutioned in the blog and said we must use CPI for it, IC would not be powerful enough). entry.title: BTP-on premises ERP connection not working
entry.content:
Hello, i am connecting my on premises system to BAS.
1. Cloud connector setup is done and connection is fine between cloud connector and On premises system . on checking connection it says - "Backend is reachable from cloud connector".

2. Configures on premises system from BAS, entered all the variables like WebIDEUsage, WebIDEEnabled, HTML5.DynamicDestination, HTML5.Timeout

3. Getting below error when opening ON premises system from BAS - "

Could not get v2 and v4 services."
4. /IWFND/CONFIG is published in /n/IWFND/V4_ADMIN
5. Tcode- /n /IWFND/ERROR_LOG is showing error - Service '/IWFND/CATALOG' version '0002' repository 'DEFAULT' not registered
6. While accessing url - https:///sap/opu/odata4/iwfnd/config/default/iwfnd/catalog/0002/ServiceGroups?$expand=DefaultSystem($expand=Services)
getting error 
"Service '/IWFND/CATALOG' version '0002' repository 'DEFAULT' not registered",

but as i mentioned /IWFND/CONFIG is already registered.

So, help me in setting up connection between BAS and On premises ERP
